title: Design durable storage tier and SLA mechanism for compliance-sensitive use cases
markdown:
## Goal

Design a "durable" storage tier or SLA mechanism that provides stronger persistence guarantees for compliance-sensitive use cases (e.g., financial records, healthcare data, legal documents). Today, B3nd nodes offer best-effort storage with no formal retention guarantees. This design defines how operators can offer and consumers can request higher durability tiers.

## Background

Current B3nd storage is uniform: all data written to a node has the same persistence characteristics determined by the backend (PostgresClient, MongoClient, MemoryClient). There is no concept of:
- Guaranteed retention periods (e.g., "keep for 7 years")
- Replication factor commitments (e.g., "store on 3+ nodes")
- Backup/snapshot policies
- Compliance attestation (proof that data was retained)

The `NodeProtocolInterface` in `libs/b3nd-core/types.ts` defines `receive()`, `read()`, `list()`, and `delete()` but has no metadata channel for storage class or retention policy. The compose system could enforce retention validators at the schema level, but no such validators exist.

The economic layer (Group 3) plans gas-based storage fees. Durable storage would command higher fees, creating a natural pricing tier. However, this design should work independently of gas -- an operator could offer durable storage as a value-added service even before tokenization.

Key design questions:
- How are durability tiers defined and advertised?
- How does a writer request a specific tier (URI convention, message metadata, separate API)?
- How does the node prove compliance (attestation, audit log, third-party verification)?
- How does deletion work for durable data (legal hold, right-to-delete conflicts)?
- How does this compose with the confirmation protocol (multi-node durability)?

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] RFC document created at `docs/design/durable-storage.md`
- [ ] Design defines at least two storage tiers (standard, durable) with clear semantics
- [ ] Design specifies how writers request a tier (URI convention or message envelope)
- [ ] Design specifies retention metadata schema (duration, replication factor, backup policy)
- [ ] Design specifies how operators configure and advertise their supported tiers
- [ ] Design specifies compliance attestation mechanism (how to prove data was retained)
- [ ] Design addresses deletion semantics for durable data (hold policies, right-to-delete)
- [ ] Design addresses interaction with the confirmation protocol for multi-node durability
- [ ] Design addresses interaction with future gas/economic layer (pricing tiers)
- [ ] Design includes draft TypeScript types for `StorageTier`, `RetentionPolicy`, `DurabilityAttestation`
- [ ] Design documents regulatory considerations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOX)

## Implementation Guide

1. Read `libs/b3nd-core/types.ts` to understand `Message`, `ReceiveResult`, `NodeProtocolInterface`, and where tier metadata could attach.

2. Read `libs/b3nd-compose/types.ts` and `libs/b3nd-compose/validators.ts` to understand how validators enforce write rules. A durability validator could check that the requested tier is supported.

3. Read `libs/b3nd-servers/http.ts` to understand the receive pipeline and where tier metadata would be extracted from requests.

4. Review `apps/b3nd-node/` to understand how a node is configured today and where tier configuration would live.

5. Create `docs/design/durable-storage.md` with the following structure:
   - **Problem statement**: Why uniform storage is insufficient for regulated industries
   - **Tier definitions**: Standard (best-effort), Durable (retention guarantee), Archive (long-term cold storage)
   - **Writer interface**: How writers specify a tier -- evaluate URI convention (e.g., `durable://`) vs message envelope metadata vs separate API parameter
   - **Operator configuration**: How node operators declare supported tiers, retention limits, and pricing
   - **Retention enforcement**: How the node ensures data is not garbage-collected before the retention period expires
   - **Replication commitments**: How multi-node replication provides durability beyond single-node backup
   - **Attestation**: Periodic proof-of-retention (signed statement that data at URI X still exists and matches hash Y)
   - **Deletion semantics**: Legal hold mechanism, right-to-delete workflow, conflict resolution
   - **Economic integration**: How durable storage commands higher gas fees (deferred to Group 3 but design should be compatible)
   - **Type signatures**: Draft interfaces for `StorageTier`, `RetentionPolicy`, `DurabilityConfig`, `RetentionAttestation`
   - **Regulatory mapping**: Brief notes on GDPR (right to erasure vs retention), HIPAA (minimum retention), SOX (7-year retention)
   - **Open questions**: Remaining design decisions

## Files to Create/Modify

- `docs/design/durable-storage.md` -- New RFC document with full durable storage design

## Testing

This is a design-only story. Verification is via review:
- All sections are substantive and internally consistent
- Type signatures are valid TypeScript
- Regulatory considerations are factually accurate
- Design is compatible with existing `NodeProtocolInterface` without breaking changes

## Dependencies

No hard prerequisites. This design should be compatible with:
- The confirmation protocol (Group 2) for multi-node durability
- The gas/economic layer (Group 3) for pricing tiers
- The offline sync design (G5-D3-04) for durable data reconciliation
tasksExcerpt:
- [ ] Design webhook/trigger support for external service integration
- [ ] Design "durable" storage tier or SLA mechanism for compliance-sensitive use cases
- [ ] Design multi-user access control beyond shared keypairs (role-based access to a namespace)
- [ ] Design offline-first sync framework (local client ↔ remote client reconciliation)

---

## E. Infrastructure & DevOps
